title: Hoochie Coochie Man
source: bitmidi.com uploads/58185 (full-length 51-measure 12/8 band transcription, track meta names Hoochie Coochie Man / Muddy Waters; replaced the 783-byte 9-measure uploads/56938 fragment, 2026-07-09 data-audit follow-up)

musicalLanguage.description: Willie Dixon wrote 'Hoochie Coochie Man' for Muddy Waters in 1954, and its stop-time riff — a swaggering figure that leaves holes for the vocal boast — became one of the defining sounds of Chicago blues. This library's transcription is a full-length 51-measure band arrangement in 12/8 at 100 BPM: 3,183 notes over roughly three minutes, with the record's vocal, harmonica, guitars, piano, bass, and drums flattened onto two staves. The form is three verse-chorus cycles, and the data shows it plainly: whole measures repeat identically sixteen bars apart (measures 12, 28, and 44; 13, 29, and 45; on through 16, 32, and 48), and the left hand's chorus accompaniment — an 86-note span at measures 11-18 — returns verbatim at 27-34 and 43-50, covering 57% of everything the left hand plays. Harmony resists a single label here: the stated A major fits the content only moderately (statedKeyFit 0.446) and the detected best guess, D major, sits at a noise-level margin of 0.051, so the honest evidence is the confidence-gated windows themselves — C major colors 25% of measures, with D and D7 at 13% each.
musicalLanguage.structure: Strophic, three cycles of stop-time verse into full-band chorus. The section lens draws boundaries at measures 5, 11, 20, 27, 36, and 43: a 4-measure intro (1-4), a stop-time verse at 5-10, the first chorus at 11-19 (novelty 1.811), verse two at 20-26, chorus two at 27-35 (novelty 2.071, the sharpest interior break), verse three at 36-42, and the final chorus at 43-51. The right hand carries a 201-note cycle spanning measures 3-19 that restates at 19-35 (67% coverage), confirming that one well-learned cycle is most of the song.
keyMoments: Measures 6-10, the stop-time verse: the confidence-gated chord windows alternate D7 in the first half of the measure with C major in the second half (measures 6, 9, and 10 all show this split), which demonstrates the riff-then-answer call-and-response that defines the song's verses.

Measures 11-18: the left hand's 86-note chorus span is stated here, then returns note-for-note at measures 27-34 and 43-50 — a model example of blues economy where one accompaniment figure, mastered once, covers 57% of the left hand's total content.

The five busiest measures (11, 12, 15, 17, and 27, each carrying 26 onsets — 14 in the right hand over 12 in the left) all land inside choruses, revealing the arrangement's dynamic plan: verses breathe around the vocal, choruses drive with the full band.

Measure 1 (12 onsets) and measure 51 (11 onsets) are among the sparsest in the file — the arrangement opens and closes at low intensity around its 26-onset chorus core, a compact lesson in framing a groove-driven song.
The right hand peaks at G6 in measure 13 while the left hand anchors on E1 from measure 1 — a five-plus-octave registral spread that shows how a full band's range gets represented on two staves.
teachingGoals: Practice the stop-time feel of measures 5-10 by clapping the rhythm first: the silences between riff statements are structural, and learning to place a hit confidently after a written gap is the core skill this song teaches.

Learn the 16-measure verse-chorus cycle as one unit and recognize its three statements (choruses landing at measures 11, 27, and 43) — the identical-measure evidence means practicing one cycle well is practicing the whole song.
Master the left-hand span of measures 11-18 before anything else: it returns verbatim at 27-34 and 43-50, so this single 86-note passage is the highest-leverage practice target in the piece.

Hear the D7-to-C major alternation inside measures 6-10 as shifting riff color rather than a settled progression: with the stated A major fitting at only 0.446 and the detected D major at a noise-level 0.051 margin, ground your ear in the windows the analysis actually certifies.
Build a genuine 12/8 groove: feel four broad beats per measure with a triplet rolling inside each one, so the shuffle swings instead of stiffening into even eighth notes.
styleTips: Leave the stop-time gaps genuinely empty — don't fill the silences in measures 5-10 with pedal haze or extra notes; the swagger lives in the space between hits.

Play the chorus peaks (the 26-onset measures around 11-17 and 27) with band weight: imagine drums and harmonica stacking in, and let your dynamic level jump at each chorus entrance rather than crescendoing gradually.
Feel the 12/8 as a rolling shuffle — think 'triplet' inside every beat and keep the eighth notes rounded, never even; at 100 BPM this should strut, not sprint.
Use a light touch on the sparse bookends (measures 1 and 51) so the arrangement's frame stays lower than its core — the song should feel like it arrives, boasts, and walks off.
Listen for the baked-in call-and-response between the vocal line and the instrumental answers in the right hand, and voice the answering figures slightly softer, the way a band defers to a singer.
